query: CanSat recovery system descent speed 8 11 m/s parachute guide

results.title: Parachute design - The CanSat book
results.description: The figure to the right shows a sketch of a plat parachute which consists of 8 equal triangles. ... You can read more about how to calculate the area of a flat parachute here: https://www.sunward1.com/imagespara/The Mathematics of Parachutes(Rev2).pdf · As soon as you know the total area (\(A\)) together with the drag coefficient (\(C_D\)) you can easily determine the descent rate for your CanSat.
results.content:
The figure to the right shows a sketch of a plat parachute which consists of 8 equal triangles. ... You can read more about how to calculate the area of a flat parachute here: https://www.sunward1.com/imagespara/The Mathematics of Parachutes(Rev2).pdf · As soon as you know the total area (\(A\)) together with the drag coefficient (\(C_D\)) you can easily determine the descent rate for your CanSat.
These pages will guide you through the different steps needed to design and built your parachute. The values listed below are preliminary and can change in advance of a CanSat competition and launch campaign. However, they can be used as a temporary guideline. Uncertainties in the Launch Campaign could lead to a different value for the eventual descent velocities.
Both will enhance the stability of the CanSat. Before we can start making the parachute we will have to figure out how big it should be. More specifically, we need to calculate how much surface area the parachute will need in order to fulfil the requirements. Logic suggests that the bigger the parachute the slower the object’s descent velocity.
Although it would be very beneficial for the CanSat to have a very low descent rate, a limit has been set to ensure that the CanSat will land near the launch area. If the descent rate is too slow the CanSat may drift kilometres away along with the wind, which is neither allowed nor desired. For safety reasons there has also been set a maximum descent rate. To design the parachute we’ll use some simple physics.

results.title: Cansat - Technické parametre - Slovak Space Office
results.description: The strength of the parachute must ... a directed landing, then a maximum of 170 seconds flight time is recommended. A descent rate between 8 and 11 m/s is recommended for recovery reasons....
results.content:
The strength of the parachute must ... a directed landing, then a maximum of 170 seconds flight time is recommended. A descent rate between 8 and 11 m/s is recommended for recovery reasons....
It is recommended to use bright coloured fabric, which will facilitate recovery of the CanSat after landing. The parachute connection must be able to withstand up to 50 N of force. The strength of the parachute must be tested to ensure that the system will operate nominally. For recovery reasons, a maximum flight time of 120 seconds is recommended. If attempting a directed landing, then a maximum of 170 seconds flight time is recommended. A descent rate between 8 and 11 m/s is recommended for recovery reasons.
All the components of the CanSat must fit inside a standard soft drinks can (115 mm height and 66 mm diameter), with the exception of the parachute. Radio antennas and GPS antennas can be mounted externally on the top or bottom of the can, depending on the design, but not on the sides.
Note: The rocket payload area usually has 4.5 cm of space per CanSat available, along the can’s axial dimension (i.e. height), which must accommodate all external elements including: parachute, parachute attachment hardware, and any antennas.
The range of allowed frequencies changes depending on the country where the event is hosted and will be communicated in due time. It is recommended that teams pay attention to the design of the CanSat in terms of hardware integration and interconnection, so the radio frequency can be easily modified if necessary.

results.title: Parachute Design and Material – CanSat Athena
results.description: To ensure the highest chance of our CanSat not taking damage and having a smooth landing, we have opted for our CanSat to descend at a terminal speed of 8.0m/s. Our final parachute design will be a flat-pack hexagon design. We have chosen this design for a few reasons.
results.content:
To ensure the highest chance of our CanSat not taking damage and having a smooth landing, we have opted for our CanSat to descend at a terminal speed of 8.0m/s. Our final parachute design will be a flat-pack hexagon design. We have chosen this design for a few reasons.
To ensure the highest chance of our CanSat not taking damage and having a smooth landing, we have opted for our CanSat to descend at a terminal speed of 8.0m/s. Our final parachute design will be a flat-pack hexagon design. We have chosen this design for a few reasons. Firstly, it is simple and relatively easy to make which allows us to focus our attention on more vital parts of the mission. Even though it is easy to make, it has a relatively high drag-coefficient which, after testing, we found to be 0.8. This is useful since our descent speed is quite low, a high drag coefficient allows us to achieve that speed with less material\area, a vital advantage when dealing with space/weight limitations.
For our parachute cord material, we chose a paracord made from Nylon with a diameter of 4mm. Due to its 11-threaded core it can withstand 3000+ Newtons , which is plenty enough for the CanSat. ... To ensure the highest chance our CanSat is recoverable, we have opted for two systems in place.
We used the drag and weight formulae to calculate the required area of the parachute: Where m is the mass of the Cansat, g is the gravitational constant, p is the local air density, v is the velocity and Cd is the drag coefficient. Assume the mass of the CanSat is 0.35kg, p is 1.225g\m3 , the velocity is 8.5m/s and the drag coefficient is 0.8.
Firstly we will use GPS to know exactly where our CanSat is, this almost guarantees the CanSat doesn’t get lost and has the added advantage we can track its flight path which is useful during testing. However, just in-case the GPS fails or for some reason the data is inaccurate, we have made our parachute and the cord a fluorescent orange colour, so it does not blend into the nature/sky, again with the added benefit of being able to easily track our flight path · Published under: Athena Decoded Parachutes/Landing

results.title: ESA - What is a CanSat?
results.description: For recovery reasons, a maximum flight time of 120 seconds is recommended. If attempting a directed landing, then a maximum of 170 seconds flight time is recommended. A descent rate between 8 and 11 m/s is recommended for recovery reasons.
results.content:
For recovery reasons, a maximum flight time of 120 seconds is recommended. If attempting a directed landing, then a maximum of 170 seconds flight time is recommended. A descent rate between 8 and 11 m/s is recommended for recovery reasons.
It is recommended to use bright coloured fabric, which will facilitate recovery of the CanSat after landing. The parachute connection must be able to withstand up to 1000 N of force. The strength of the parachute must be tested to ensure that the system will operate nominally. For recovery reasons, a maximum flight time of 120 seconds is recommended. If attempting a directed landing, then a maximum of 170 seconds flight time is recommended. A descent rate between 8 and 11 m/s is recommended for recovery reasons.
N.B. The rocket payload area has 4.5 cm of space per CanSat available, along the can’s axial dimension (i.e. height), which must accommodate all external elements including: parachute, parachute attachment hardware, and any antennas.
All the components of the CanSat must fit inside a standard soda can (115 mm height and 66 mm diameter), with the exception of the parachute.
A CanSat is a simulation of a real satellite, integrated within the volume and shape of a soft drink can. The challenge for the students is to fit all the major subsystems found in a satellite, such as power, sensors and a communication system, into this minimal volume.

results.title: The recovery system – KrakSat
results.description: The expected descent speed at the weight of 350 g will be about 9 m/s, giving the estimated flight time of about 360 seconds. To integrate the CanSat with the carrier rocket the parachute has to be folded.
results.content:
The expected descent speed at the weight of 350 g will be about 9 m/s, giving the estimated flight time of about 360 seconds. To integrate the CanSat with the carrier rocket the parachute has to be folded.
How to fold a parachute.

results.title: Nordic CanSat Competition Guidelines | Nordic ESERO | Using Space in Education
results.description: The deployable subsystems and recovery ... time is limited to 120 sec (If not other agreements have been made). The descent rate must be between 8 m/s and 11m/s (If not other agreements have been made)....
results.content:
The deployable subsystems and recovery ... time is limited to 120 sec (If not other agreements have been made). The descent rate must be between 8 m/s and 11m/s (If not other agreements have been made)....
The deployable subsystems and recovery system can exceed the length of the primary structure, but can’t be deployed before the CanSat is fully ejected from the rocket. Flight time is limited to 120 sec (If not other agreements have been made). The descent rate must be between 8 m/s and 11m/s (If not other agreements have been made).
In addition to the space theme, there is a list of technical requirements for the CanSat which has to be passed before flight (for example weight and size) and also some requirements during flight (for example decent speed). All the components (with the exception of parachute, antenna and GPS) of the CanSat must fit inside a European soda can: 115 mm height and 66 mm diameter.
During the CanSat operation, each team will get their CanSats launched up to an altitude of approximately 1 000 meter by a rocket. At this altitude the CanSats will be ejected from the rocket. During the descent, the CanSats will perform their missions and send data to the ground trough a radio transmitter.
The CanSat must be able to withstand an acceleration of up to 20G.

results.title: CanSat 2021 - Herotech
results.description: The printed parts are secured to the rest of the CanSat in 4 points: 2 by the M2 rod and 2 by pressing the sides in the outer walls, providing a tight fit and ease of removal. To each one of the motors, 3D printed pulleys are attached that allow the length of the strings of the parachute to be completely controlled. With this in mind, we can completely steer the parachute, which is a paraglider, by pulling on the side strings, or even slowing the descent rate by pulling all the strings simultaneously.
results.content:
The printed parts are secured to the rest of the CanSat in 4 points: 2 by the M2 rod and 2 by pressing the sides in the outer walls, providing a tight fit and ease of removal. To each one of the motors, 3D printed pulleys are attached that allow the length of the strings of the parachute to be completely controlled. With this in mind, we can completely steer the parachute, which is a paraglider, by pulling on the side strings, or even slowing the descent rate by pulling all the strings simultaneously.
We are Herotech, a team of 4 passionate and young students that believe that things can be done differently. We dream for a better future, a more efficient way to use everyday objects, a method to make life just a little bit different. We strive for robotic assistance, for a future in which our human barriers can be overcome.
The printed parts are secured to the rest of the CanSat in 4 points :2 by the M2 rod and 2 by pressing the sides in the outer walls, providing a tight fit and ease of removal. To each one of the motors, 3D printed pulleys are attached that allow the length of the strings of the parachute to be completely controlled. With this in minde, we can completely steer the parachute, which is a paraglider, by pulling on the side strings, or even slowing the descent rate by pulling all the strings simultaneously.
The mission we have chosen consists in a guided airdrop system. The CanSat should be able to guide itself during the fall by pulling the strings attached to the parachute with the target of landing in a nearby area relative to the dropping point. This presents a great potential in the development of an air drop package delivering system for faster and more convenient delivery which does not require human interaction.

results.title: Technology | T10 teach with space teacher guide & student worksheets
results.description: According to the competition guidelines for CanSat, a few seconds after deploying its parachute (i.e. after launch), it should have a descent rate (terminal velocity) between 8 – 11 m/s.
results.content:
According to the competition guidelines for CanSat, a few seconds after deploying its parachute (i.e. after launch), it should have a descent rate (terminal velocity) between 8 – 11 m/s.

results.title: ESA - Design your parachute – A Guide to Landing Your CanSat Safely | Teach with Space T10
results.description: Students will learn about the underlying physics of parachutes and their design and how to control the speed of their CanSat.
results.content:
Students will learn about the underlying physics of parachutes and their design and how to control the speed of their CanSat.
Appreciate why it is important to test a parachute. Understand the concept of terminal velocity. Represent graphs with corrects units and labels. This classroom resource is part of a set of resources developed by ESA’s Education Office in collaboration with the European Space Education Resource Offices (ESEROs) to support the European CanSat Competition.
